speaker: interviewer
text: --Angeles. Go ahead, Mike.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where were we?

speaker: interviewer
text: We were with the [? master ?] when you told him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I told him-- I asked the gu-- I asked the man. I says-- I says, why? Why am I here? What have I done wrong? Didn't say anything. But I remember it was about-- about eight days or ten days before we got liberated. I-- we felt that we're going to get liberated soon because he, the man, used to tell me sometimes, [NON-ENGLISH] not for long. Not for long. Not for long at all. Hold on to-- Hold on. Hold on. Hold on. He told me himself. He told himself.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the end of-- before the war, we took-- before the liberation, and that [INAUDIBLE] before they took us back, away from [NON-ENGLISH]. He used to say, I have a nice daughter for you. After the war, I want you to come live with me. I says, why can't I come live with you now? Why do I have to wait until I be se-- liberated from someone else? Why don't you try to take me out of here?

speaker: interviewee
text: He says that's-- he didn't say anything. He probably couldn't or he wouldn't. Whatever had happened, I don't know. So anyway, I never saw that man anymore and I never cared for him anyway. And, uh-- but the, uh-- but the, uh, liberation was something to-- to remember. And I will remember this for the rest of my life. And the reason why I do this interview is to say-- to tell our kids and great grandchildren what we went through. What could happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about your two--

speaker: interviewee
text: What could happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about happy times. Tell us about your two daughters.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have two beautiful daughters. And I-- no. Don't interrupt me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their names first.

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to say that. I want to--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to have this tape, and people should see it. For our children, and our great grandchildren, and great grandchildren. When they will come across something like that, they should try to destroy it from the beginning, not to let it happen again because it is just getting worse and worse. And I'm saying this to my children and to my-- all people, even Jewish or not Jewish. If something happened, things like that that try to-- to destroy you, try to destroy it first before it develops in something big. And that's why we're doing the tape. It should be for generations to come.

speaker: interviewer
text: I agree with you Mike. But tell us one thing. Tell us now about your two daughters. Tell us about happy times now. I know you had a tragedy. Your wife died.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But tell us about your two beautiful daughters.

speaker: interviewee
text: We moved-- we moved here to Los Angeles. And it's starting to go all-- every day, better and better and better since we moved here to Los Angeles. We had a happy life here-- here, me and my wife and my two children. We used to have a lot of people all the time in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: The name of your two daughters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like the name of your two daughters. Medelein Dix and Judy Gluck-- Judy Nelson, our two dau-- my two daughters. One is 43 and one is 39. One-- my younger daughter gave me two beautiful grandchildren, which I'm very proud of. And their name.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, uh, their name is Jashua and one is Eric. And, uh, as a matter of fact, my-- my, uh, older grandson now is accepted to UCLA, which he made me proud again. I mean, he's a very bright boy, and a very-- very smart boy and a good student. And my younger one, my younger grandson is into sport, and he's a rascal, whatever you call a rascal. He's, uh, the boy that I like, actually. A boy from boys. And, uh, my two daughters give me a lot of pleasure. Every family goes through a lot of problems, which I had a little problem with my older daughter, which I wouldn't go into. But I was very much for it that they should marry Jewish men, which they did. And I have a lot of nachas from both of them, from my two grandsons and also from my two son-in-laws, also. They're wonderful two people, good providers. They take care-- good care of my two daughters, which makes me proud of them too. As a matter of fact, we are very close together. And we see each other now and then. We go out to lunch, and take them out to lunch. And when there is a birthday, we celebrate the birthday. And as a matter of fact, last week I was out with my little grandson, which I take him out once a week sometimes. And we go out and, you know, how a grandfather is a spoils.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there is happy times afterwards.

speaker: interviewee
text: There are happy times, yeah. And, uh, he knows that he can get away with me about anything. Can anything he wants, he gets. And then before I said goodbye to him last week, he hugged me, and he said, Zaide, you're a good zaide.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's grandpa. Listen, and then you married again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then my wife unfortunate passed away. And we had a lot of struggle with that. And I was hurt for a very long time because we really had a life like Romeo and Juliet. We respected each other very much, and we had a wonderful life. But you know how it is. God sometimes doesn't let you have a good time all the time. But he must do something.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why it happened to her, I don't know, because she was just like an angel. Never done anything wrong to anybody. And she wanted to live so much because she had a lot to live for. Her two grandsons was her life, you know? So therefore, every Friday night, we used to have a big dinner here. And the families to get together. And now, but unfortunately, she passed away. And after a few years, I did realize that's not good to be alone. There got to be somebody in your life. So I met my wonderful wife now. It so happened that we-- we met in a-- in a single dance. Her name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: And her name is Shirley, and she makes me very happy. And, uh, we have a good life.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I hope you stay happy for the rest of your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I hope we stay happy all our life, yeah. And she's-- she's a good wife. And she's a very, very beautiful lady. And she makes me proud. And she has a beautiful family herself. She has three children of her own-- two daughters and a son. And, uh, we are very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm glad you found happiness again, Michael.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and we're very close. As a matter of fact, um, we went out and we took out her little granddaughter. She's-- her name is Kylie. We had such a good time. And we looked at the pictures, one [INAUDIBLE] her book. And [INAUDIBLE] into my house and I was sitting in a chair. And she wanted to push yourself into the chair with me, to sit in the chair with me, in the same chair. So we have-- we have a very good relationship. And, uh, you know, and, uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: But that's the most important thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: She has a wonderful mom and dad. My wife has a mother and father. And then as a matter of fact, up to 120, well, what, a few weeks ago, we celebrated their 60th wedding anniversary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tremendous.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which it's a [NON-ENGLISH] It's a [NON-ENGLISH] from God to have that. So the family-- I'm very happy with the family. And I'm trying to be a good husband too, which I always have been, and a good father and a good grandfather and as much as I could. And you see? So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Michael, do you have any pictures from before the war? Or any--

speaker: interviewee
text: I have only one picture from my dad there, on the wall. And I haven't got no pictures from my mom, and I don't have any pictures from my sister, which I regret that very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whatever-- you told me when I did the pre-interview that you had a older brother. What happened to--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had two older brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two older brothers.

speaker: interviewee
text: One was in Romania, which he was not deported. He was not deported. Very, very religious, with beards up to here. And one brother I had in Czech-- used to be Czechoslovakian. By that time, it became Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were that your real brothers or your half brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: My half brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your half brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: From one father, yes. We had the same father. So and as a matter of fact, I'm going now October the seventh, I'm going to one of my brother's son's son marries-- gets married. And I'm invited. Just yesterday, we got the invitation. We're going to go to Bridgeport, Connecticut. Me and my wife, we're going to go to the wedding. And I hope we're going to have a--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's your brother's grandson?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother's grandson, yes. He is-- now we're going to the wedding. It's October the 7th. And I hope-- and he says, uncle, I am sure, and I'm sure I'm going to give you the best time you have.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your brother's name? Those two brothers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fishel. One was [NON-ENGLISH] and one was Itzek Fishel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Itzek Fishel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very, very religious people. As a matter of fact, they were both [NON-ENGLISH] .

speaker: interviewer
text: From two different mothers.

speaker: interviewee
text: From two different-- yeah, they were from my daddy's first, uh, wife. And I from my dad's second wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: You and your sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: My and my sister. Me and my sister, yes. As a matter of fact--

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister's name? What was your sister's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pearl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pearl.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was a beautiful, beautiful girl, a beautiful girl. I always when I look at ladies that they have black hair like you, beautiful hair and just that she would have been just like Elizabeth Taylor. I mean, just the kind of face she had. That's a beautiful girl. She was a beautiful girl. She perished.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't have any pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I have no pictures at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Mike. Now we're going to do-- you know what we're going to do-- [AUDIO OUT]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father, and he died in 1942. When he was born, I don't remember. I don't know when he was born. But he was one of the finest men, and a very religious man. And he was a little tailor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Michael, excuse me. Can we know his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Michael-- his name was a Mechal. Mechal. And also I said that he died in 1942. And he-- what he, uh-- what he did, he was a little tailor. And, uh, he was in the United States in 1904 to 1911. And he came back because everything was [? stray ?] for him, was not kosher enough. So he came home. And I remember that he brought a sewing machine that he used to for his tailor shop. He bought it from the United States of America. So this is the machine he used to use to till-- till the end of time. So, uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Michael.

speaker: interviewee
text: And therefore, [? never saw-- ?] On the right, this is my daughter Judy, my younger daughter when she got married. And after it's me and my wife, my late wife, and my older daughter. And she got married in 1976. And was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Their names.

speaker: interviewee
text: And theirs names is Judy. My name is Mike. And my wife is name is Rosa. And my older daughter is Medelein.

speaker: interviewer
text: Medelein.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And then was the wedding in 1976. It was a very happy occasion. We had a beautiful wedding. And, uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was the wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Temple Beth El.

speaker: interviewer
text: Here in Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Los Angeles. It's in Crescent Heights and Fountain. The first one on the right side-- on my right side is my older grandson. His name is Josh.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That picture was taken in 1994, May the 22nd. My second marriage now to Shirley, which is all the family's here. And this is beginning of my right side is my older grandson, Josh, and my younger grandson is Eric. And then it's my son-in-law. That means their father is Ronnie. And then my younger daughter, which is their mother, is my-- her name is Judy. And then after that, that comes in the back is my son-in-law, by name Elliott, my older daughter Medelein. And then it's me, of course and my bride, of course. And then it starts her three beautiful children. Her son, his name is Philip. And then his-- her daughter is Allie. And her beautiful little granddaughter-- our granddaughter-- Kylie. And her husband, Beau. and her younger daughter, Clara, which we're all a happy family. And I hope this to be a happy family for a long, long time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So do I.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's amen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Mike.

speaker: interviewee
text: Amen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
